ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Bún Riêu. FOODON_00002473 (noodle soup food product) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP (Soups and broths) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for Vietnamese crab paste vermicelli soup.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this specific dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Bún Riêu as served in a Singapore Vietnamese restaurant typically weighs approximately 500 g, comprising broth, rice vermicelli, crab balls, fish cake, shrimp, tofu, and garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: Bun Rieu / Vietnamese crab noodle soup — estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data and USDA FoodData Central component values for rice vermicelli soup with seafood and pork
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table; USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Bún Riêu exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA tables. Values are estimated by component analysis: rice vermicelli soup base (~55 kcal/100 g), crab/prawn/pork protein contribution, tofu and fish cake fat contribution, tomato-based broth, and shrimp paste sodium. Composite estimate cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central entries for Vietnamese-style seafood noodle soups and pho-type broths. Serving-level values reflect a 500 g bowl.

gi_source.title: Rice vermicelli GI reference — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281–2283; University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice noodles/vermicelli
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bún Riêu as a composite dish. GI of 53 is estimated based on the dominant carbohydrate source: rice vermicelli (GI ~53 per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international tables). The protein, fat, and fibre from crab, pork, tofu, and broth components lower the effective GI of the complete dish. GL per serving calculated as: (53 × 34.0 g carbohydrate per 500 g serving) / 100 = 18.0, rounded to 18.2 accounting for mixed-dish dilution. gi_category is low (GI ≤ 55).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from shrimp paste, fish sauce, and seasoned broth
hypertension.guidance: A single 500 g bowl of Bún Riêu provides approximately 2100 mg sodium, which exceeds the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced seasoning, limit broth consumption, and monitor total daily sodium intake carefully.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice vermicelli carbohydrates, partially offset by protein and fat from seafood, pork, and tofu
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 53 (low) and a glycemic load of approximately 18 per bowl, Bún Riêu can be consumed in moderation by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; portion control of the vermicelli component and pairing with the protein-rich toppings will help attenuate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from pork and crab components; beneficial omega-3 contribution from seafood
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (~2100 mg per bowl) poses a significant cardiovascular risk for individuals with existing heart disease; however, the seafood components (crab, shrimp, prawn) provide beneficial lean protein and omega-3 fatty acids, and individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit broth intake and choose leaner protein toppings where possible.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bún Riêu (Crab Paste and Pork Vermicelli Soup) is a traditional Vietnamese noodle soup that has been adopted into the Singapore food scene, particularly at Vietnamese restaurants such as Co Chung Restaurant. The dish features a rich, tangy broth made primarily from prawn, pork bones, and ripe tomatoes, seasoned with fermented crab paste (mắm cua) and shrimp paste, which gives the soup its characteristic reddish-orange colour and deeply savoury, umami-forward flavour. The soup is served over thin rice vermicelli (bún) and topped with a mixed crab ball (a patty of minced crab meat and egg), fish cake slices, whole shrimp, and soft tofu cubes. Fresh garnishes such as bean sprouts, perilla, water spinach, and lime are typically served on the side. Nutritionally, Bún Riêu is a moderate-energy dish (~310 kcal per 500 g bowl) with a good protein content (~26 g per bowl) from its diverse seafood and pork components. The dish is high in sodium due to the fermented pastes and seasoned broth, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is low (~53), driven by the rice vermicelli base, and the glycemic load per bowl is moderate (~18), making it suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when portion size is controlled. The dish contains crustacean, fish, and soy allergens and is not suitable for halal or vegetarian diets.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Bún Riêu exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA primary tables. Nutrient values are estimated by component analysis using FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for constituent ingredients (rice vermicelli, crab, pork, prawn, tofu, tomato broth). GI is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate source (rice vermicelli) using the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated; no single approved-source entry for Bún Riêu as a complete dish was found. Values should be validated against a laboratory analysis or an HPB Singapore Foodo entry if one becomes available. 2. Sodium estimate (~420 mg/100 g) is based on typical Vietnamese noodle soup broth seasoned with shrimp paste and fish sauce; actual restaurant values may vary significantly. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA SR Legacy component sources used for this estimation. 4. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used for this estimation. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used for this estimation. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in the component sources (no industrial hydrogenated fats in traditional recipe). 7. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate based on rice vermicelli as the dominant glycemic carbohydrate source per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). 8. The dish is classified under Singapore cuisine as it is served by a Singapore restaurant (Co Chung Restaurant, Lorong Telok), though its origin is Vietnamese; multilingual names follow Singaporean dish rules (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) with Vietnamese name also included. 9. is_halal is false as the dish contains pork.
